content.assessment: The Polish case is highly relevant to this proposal's core rationale: Poland's 2017 reversal is a paradigmatic example of a structural decision made on explicitly political/populist grounds while disregarding substantial existing evidence (positive PISA outcomes, labour-market data) supporting the prior reform — precisely the failure mode a national evidence/transparency registry is designed to prevent or at least expose. This lens strongly validates the proposal's diagnosis that evidence-light, top-down political reversals cause severe disruption (closure of ~7,000 schools, ~10,000 teachers displaced, thousands of unplaced students), and that better information infrastructure might have made such a reversal harder to execute without scrutiny, though it offers no evidence that transparency infrastructure alone would have prevented a politically motivated decision. The proposal's own identified risk — that an information-only framework may be ignored given historical patterns of top-down override — is directly corroborated by the Polish 2017 case, which proceeded despite available evidence.
opportunities: A Hungarian registry tracking realized outcomes and costs could, if institutionalized with sufficient independence, create a harder-to-ignore evidentiary record than existed in Poland's case, though this remains speculative
strengths: Poland's 2017 reversal directly demonstrates the failure mode this proposal targets: political decisions overriding available evidence (PISA outcomes, labour-market data) (F-live-polish_reform-04)

The severe disruption costs of that reversal (7,000 school closures, 10,000 teachers, unplaced student cohorts) illustrate the material stakes of decisions made without institutionalized evidence use, reinforcing the case for registry-based tracking (F-live-polish_reform-05)
threats: The proposal's own identified risk — that information-only frameworks may be ignored — is directly corroborated by Poland's 2017 experience, where political rationale prevailed despite available evidence (F-live-polish_reform-04)
weaknesses: Poland's case shows that even where evidence existed and was presumably visible, it did not prevent a politically motivated reversal, suggesting an evidence/transparency registry may be necessary but insufficient to prevent similar outcomes
